does: This paper investigates how large language models process emotional valence using mechanistic interpretability techniques. The authors apply activation patching and steering methods to open-source LLMs to trace how negative and positive emotional content flows through network layers. They discover asymmetric processing: negative valence activations concentrate in early layers while positive valence peaks in mid-to-late layers. By manipulating prompts with fixed topics but flipped emotional valence, they demonstrate these representations are causally important rather than mere surface-level token artifacts. The work further shows that identified valence-encoding directions can be steered to shift neutral prompts toward positive sentiment, establishing that emotional content exists as a manipulable, localized property within the model's computational structure.
connects: This work extends recent advances in mechanistic interpretability that have mapped how specific concepts and features are encoded throughout transformer networks. It builds on activation patching and causal intervention methods previously applied to study factual knowledge, reasoning, and linguistic features. The paper responds to the broader question of how LLMs represent abstract semantic properties beyond factual content, paralleling work on bias, toxicity, and other value-laden concepts in neural networks. It connects to research examining whether models rely on surface-level pattern matching or develop structured internal representations, and to steering and control methods that aim to manipulate model behavior through identified interpretable directions. The asymmetric-depth finding resonates with observations that different linguistic and conceptual properties emerge at different layers of transformer models.
enables: This work opens pathways for interpretability-driven alignment and oversight of LLMs by identifying emotional valence as a concrete, manipulable target. The localized and causal nature of valence processing could support techniques for controlling model outputs related to sentiment, tone, and emotional framing—critical for safe deployment. Future work might extend this framework to other abstract concepts (uncertainty, sarcasm, tone) to build a more complete map of value-laden reasoning in LLMs. The steering capability suggests potential for mechanistic approaches to reducing harmful outputs or amplifying desired communicative properties without retraining. Additionally, understanding asymmetric valence processing could inform architectural improvements or training methods that better align emotional reasoning with human values, and enable more fine-grained evaluation of model behavior beyond surface-level metrics.
